  • Enable WP_DEBUG to turn the vague 500 into a specific, fixable error.
  • Work through the fixes in order rather than guessing.

Before you start: back up and enable debugging

Always take a full backup before editing core files. Then turn on WordPress debugging so the vague 500 error reveals its real cause. Add this to wp-config.php:

define( 'WP_DEBUG', true );
define( 'WP_DEBUG_LOG', true );
define( 'WP_DEBUG_DISPLAY', false );

Errors are then logged to wp-content/debug.log, which often names the exact plugin, theme, or file causing the problem.

1. Fix a corrupted .htaccess file

A broken .htaccess file is the most common cause of a 500 error. To fix it, connect via FTP or your host's file manager, rename .htaccess to .htaccess_old, and reload your site. If it works, the file was the problem. Then go to Settings → Permalinks and click Save to generate a fresh one, or paste in the default WordPress rules:

# BEGIN WordPress
<IfModule mod_rewrite.c>
RewriteEngine On
RewriteBase /
RewriteRule ^index\.php$ - [L]
RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME} !-f
RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME} !-d
RewriteRule . /index.php [L]
</IfModule>
# END WordPress

2. Increase the PHP memory limit

Running out of PHP memory frequently triggers a 500 error. Raise the limit by adding this to wp-config.php:

define( 'WP_MEMORY_LIMIT', '256M' );

If that doesn't hold, edit php.ini (or ask your host) to set memory_limit = 256M. A memory-related 500 often appears right after installing a heavy plugin or theme.

3. Deactivate all plugins

A faulty or incompatible plugin is a leading cause. If you can access the dashboard, deactivate all plugins at once, then reactivate them one by one, reloading the site after each until the error returns — that's your culprit. If you're locked out, use FTP to rename the wp-content/plugins folder to plugins_old, which deactivates them all, then restore and test individually.

4. Switch to a default theme

If plugins aren't the cause, your theme might be. Activate a default theme like Twenty Twenty-Four. If you can't reach the dashboard, rename your active theme's folder in wp-content/themes via FTP — WordPress will fall back to a default theme automatically. If the error clears, the theme is the problem.

5. Re-upload core WordPress files

Corrupted core files can cause a 500 error. Download a fresh copy of WordPress from wordpress.org, then via FTP re-upload the wp-admin and wp-includes folders, overwriting the existing ones. This replaces any damaged core files without touching your content in wp-content or your database.

6. Check file and folder permissions

Incorrect permissions can trigger a 500 error. The standard, secure values are:

  • Folders: 755
  • Files: 644
  • wp-config.php: 600 or 640

Avoid ever setting anything to 777 — it's insecure and some servers reject it, causing the very error you're trying to fix.

7. Check your PHP version and server logs

An outdated or incompatible PHP version can cause 500 errors, especially after a plugin or theme update. Make sure you're on a supported version (PHP 8.0+). Finally, check your server error logs (via your hosting panel) — for a persistent 500 error, the log usually contains the specific PHP fatal error you need to fix.

Frequently asked questions

What causes a 500 Internal Server Error in WordPress?

The most common causes are a corrupted .htaccess file, an exhausted PHP memory limit, and plugin or theme conflicts. Less often, it's caused by corrupted core files, incorrect file permissions, or an incompatible PHP version. Because 500 is a generic error, you find the cause by testing each in turn.

How do I fix a 500 error if I can't access wp-admin?

Use FTP or your host's file manager. Rename the .htaccess file to force a fresh one, rename the wp-content/plugins folder to deactivate all plugins, or rename your theme folder to fall back to a default theme. These let you isolate the cause without needing the dashboard.

How do I see the real error behind a 500 error?

Enable debugging by adding WP_DEBUG and WP_DEBUG_LOG to your wp-config.php, then check wp-content/debug.log. You can also review your server's error logs in your hosting control panel. Both usually reveal the specific plugin, theme, or PHP error causing the generic 500 message.

Can a plugin cause a 500 Internal Server Error?

Yes — a faulty, outdated, or incompatible plugin is one of the most common causes. Deactivate all plugins (via the dashboard or by renaming the plugins folder over FTP), then reactivate them one at a time to find which one triggers the error.

Final thoughts

A 500 Internal Server Error looks scary, but it's rarely serious once you know where to look. Back up first, enable debugging to uncover the real message, then work through the fixes in order: reset .htaccess, raise PHP memory, rule out plugins and themes, and check core files, permissions, and PHP version. One of these resolves the vast majority of 500 errors — and with debugging on, you'll usually know exactly which one before you even start.

blog_posts.title: WordPress Memory Limit Error Solution
blog_posts.excerpt: Fix the WordPress "Allowed memory size exhausted" error by increasing the PHP memory limit — via wp-config.php, php.ini, .htaccess, or your host. Recommended value, causes, and FAQ.
blog_posts.content:
If your site suddenly shows "Fatal error: Allowed memory size of XXXXXX bytes exhausted" — or a blank page after uploading an image or activating a plugin — you've hit the WordPress memory limit error. It means PHP ran out of the memory it's allowed to use. The good news: it's one of the easiest WordPress errors to fix, and you have several ways to do it. Here's the complete solution.

Quick answer: Fix the WordPress memory limit error by increasing PHP's memory allocation. Add define( 'WP_MEMORY_LIMIT', '256M' ); to wp-config.php. If that isn't enough, also raise memory_limit in php.ini or .htaccess, or ask your host to increase it. Most sites run comfortably at 256M.

What is the WordPress memory limit error?

Every WordPress site runs on PHP, and PHP is given a fixed amount of memory (RAM) to build each page. The memory limit caps how much a single script can use. When a plugin, theme, or process needs more than that cap, PHP stops and throws the "allowed memory size exhausted" error — sometimes as a visible message, sometimes as a blank white screen.

The typical error looks like this:

Fatal error: Allowed memory size of 67108864 bytes exhausted
(tried to allocate 2097152 bytes) in /home/site/public_html/wp-includes/...

That 67108864 is 64MB — a common (and often too low) default.

What causes it?

  • A low default memory limit from your host (often 64MB or less).
  • Heavy plugins — page builders, WooCommerce, backup and import tools.
  • Large image uploads that need memory to process and resize.
  • Many plugins running at once, each adding to the total.
  • Import or migration tasks that process a lot of data in one request.

Solution 1: Increase the limit in wp-config.php (easiest)

This is the first thing to try and it fixes most cases. Open wp-config.php in your site's root folder (via your host's file manager or FTP) and add this line above the "That's all, stop editing!" comment:

define( 'WP_MEMORY_LIMIT', '256M' );

Save, then reload your site. If you also hit the limit inside the admin area (during imports or updates), raise the admin limit too:

define( 'WP_MAX_MEMORY_LIMIT', '512M' );

Note: WP_MEMORY_LIMIT can only raise the limit up to what your server allows. If your host caps PHP memory below this, use Solution 2 or 3.

Solution 2: Increase memory_limit in php.ini

If editing wp-config.php doesn't stick, change PHP's setting directly. Many hosts expose PHP settings in the control panel (cPanel → "Select PHP Version" / "PHP Options" / "MultiPHP INI Editor") where you can set:

memory_limit = 256M

If you have access to a php.ini file in your site root, add the same line there. This sets the ceiling that WP_MEMORY_LIMIT is allowed to reach.

Solution 3: Increase it via .htaccess

On Apache servers, you can raise the limit in your .htaccess file (in the site root). Add:

php_value memory_limit 256M

Only use this if your host allows php_value directives — on some servers it causes a 500 error, in which case simply remove the line and use another solution.

Solution 4: Ask your host (or upgrade hosting)

Some managed and shared hosts lock PHP memory at the server level, so none of the above will exceed their cap. In that case, contact your host and ask them to raise your memory_limit to 256M — a reasonable request they can usually do in seconds. If you're constantly hitting limits on cheap shared hosting, it's a sign to move to a better plan or a quality WordPress host.

How to check your current memory limit

Not sure what you're working with? WordPress shows it for you. Go to Tools → Site Health → Info → Server and look for "PHP memory limit." Check it before and after your change to confirm the new value took effect.

Which value should you use?

Memory limit Good for
64M Bare minimum — often too low, causes errors
128M Simple blogs and small sites
256M Recommended default for most sites
512M+ WooCommerce, large sites, heavy imports

Don't set it absurdly high "just in case" — an unusually high limit can hide a memory leak or a badly coded plugin that's the real problem. 256M is the sweet spot for most sites.

Still getting the error? Find the real cause

If you've raised the limit and it still crashes, something is consuming too much memory. Track it down:

  • Deactivate plugins one by one — find the memory-hungry one.
  • Switch to a default theme temporarily to rule out theme code.
  • Enable WP_DEBUG to log the exact file and line hitting the limit.
  • Check for a plugin loop or leak — a badly coded plugin can consume memory endlessly no matter how high you set the cap.

Frequently asked questions

How do I increase the memory limit in WordPress?

Add define( 'WP_MEMORY_LIMIT', '256M' ); to your wp-config.php file, above the "stop editing" line. If your host caps PHP memory lower, also set memory_limit = 256M in php.ini or your hosting control panel, or ask your host to raise it.

What is a good memory limit for WordPress?

256M is the recommended memory limit for most WordPress sites. Simple blogs can run on 128M, while WooCommerce stores, large sites, and heavy import tasks may need 512M or more. WordPress itself recommends at least 128M.

Why does my WordPress memory limit keep getting exhausted?

If the error returns even after raising the limit, a plugin or theme is consuming excessive memory — often a page builder, backup tool, or a badly coded or leaking plugin. Deactivate plugins one by one and enable WP_DEBUG to find the exact cause instead of just raising the cap higher.

Can increasing the memory limit break my site?

No. Raising the PHP memory limit to a reasonable value like 256M is safe and simply lets your site use more memory when it needs it. The only caveat is that a php_value line in .htaccess can cause a 500 error on some servers — if that happens, remove the line and use another method.

Final thought

The WordPress memory limit error looks scary but is genuinely simple to fix: give PHP more memory. Start with WP_MEMORY_LIMIT in wp-config.php, move up to php.ini or your host if needed, and settle on 256M for most sites. And if it keeps coming back, don't just keep raising the number — find the plugin that's eating your memory and fix the root cause.

Fighting a memory error that won't go away? Get in touch and I'll find what's draining your site's memory and fix it properly.

blog_posts.title: Cloudflare Cache Problems and Solutions
blog_posts.excerpt: Fix the most common Cloudflare caching issues — stale content after updates, cached cart/checkout pages, CSS/JS not refreshing, low cache hit ratio, and cached errors — with the exact purge steps, cache rules, and a best-practice setup, plus an FAQ.
blog_posts.content:
Most Cloudflare cache problems come down to five issues: stale content after you update your site, dynamic pages (cart, checkout, login) being cached, CSS/JS changes not showing, a low cache hit ratio, or cached error and redirect pages. Nearly all of them are fixed by purging the cache correctly and setting the right page rules so Cloudflare caches static files but never dynamic ones. This guide covers each problem and its exact solution.

Cloudflare's cache is what makes your site fast — but when it serves the wrong version of a page, it can be confusing and frustrating. Here's how to diagnose what's happening and fix it.

How Cloudflare caching works (in one minute)

Cloudflare stores copies of your static files — images, CSS, JavaScript, fonts — on its global network and serves them from the data center closest to each visitor. By default it does not cache HTML unless you tell it to. Understanding this matters, because most "cache problems" are really about the wrong content being cached or an old copy not being cleared.

You can see what's happening by checking the cf-cache-status response header in your browser's Network tab:

  • HIT — served from Cloudflare's cache.
  • MISS — fetched from your server, then cached.
  • BYPASS — intentionally not cached.
  • DYNAMIC — not eligible for caching (default for HTML).

Common Cloudflare cache problems and their solutions

1. You updated your site but still see the old version

The most common complaint. Cloudflare (or your browser) is serving a cached copy from before your change.

Solution: Purge the Cloudflare cache. In the dashboard go to Caching → Configuration → Purge Cache. Use Purge Everything after a big change, or Purge by URL for a single page. Also hard-refresh your browser (Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + R) to rule out local caching. If you use WordPress, the official Cloudflare plugin can purge automatically when you update content.

2. Cart, checkout, or login pages behave strangely

Dynamic pages should never be cached. If Cloudflare caches them, users see other people's sessions, empty carts, or failed checkouts.

Solution: Create a Cache Rule (or page rule) to Bypass Cache for /cart/*, /checkout/*, /my-account/*, and /wp-admin/*. Cloudflare also automatically bypasses caching when certain cookies (like WordPress login or WooCommerce session cookies) are present — make sure those aren't being stripped.

3. CSS or JavaScript changes aren't showing up

After a design tweak, the site still loads old styles because the CSS/JS files are cached with a long expiry.

Solution: Purge the cache, then use cache-busting — append a version query string (e.g. style.css?v=2) so the browser and Cloudflare treat it as a new file. If you enabled Auto Minify or Rocket Loader and something looks broken, disable them and re-test.

4. Low cache hit ratio (Cloudflare isn't caching enough)

If most requests show MISS or DYNAMIC, Cloudflare is passing traffic to your server instead of serving from cache, so you lose the speed benefit.

Solution: Add a Cache Rule to "Cache Everything" for your static, non-personalized pages, and set an appropriate Edge Cache TTL. Make sure your server isn't sending no-cache or private cache-control headers on assets. Check the Caching analytics in the dashboard to measure improvement.

5. Cloudflare cached an error page or a redirect loop

Sometimes a temporary 500/404 error or a bad redirect gets cached and keeps showing even after you fix the underlying issue.

Solution: Purge the affected URL(s). For redirect loops, check your SSL/TLS mode — using "Flexible" when your host already has SSL is the classic cause; switch to Full (Strict). By default Cloudflare doesn't cache most error responses, so purging usually clears it.

6. Changes work for you but not for other visitors (or vice versa)

This points to a caching layer somewhere, not a code problem.

Solution: Remember there are usually three caches: your browser, your WordPress/caching plugin, and Cloudflare. Clear all three in order. Enable Cloudflare Development Mode (Caching → Configuration) to temporarily bypass the edge cache for three hours while you actively work — then turn it off.

Best-practice caching setup to prevent problems

  • Cache static assets (images, CSS, JS, fonts) with a long Edge Cache TTL.
  • Never cache cart, checkout, account, admin, or any personalized page.
  • Purge automatically on content updates using the Cloudflare plugin or API.
  • Use versioned asset URLs so updates bust the cache automatically.
  • Set SSL to Full (Strict) to avoid redirect-loop caching issues.
  • Monitor cf-cache-status and the Caching analytics to confirm it's working.

Frequently asked questions

How do I clear the Cloudflare cache?

In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to Caching → Configuration → Purge Cache. Use "Purge Everything" for site-wide changes or "Purge by URL" for a single page. On WordPress, the official Cloudflare plugin can purge automatically whenever you update content.

Why does my site still show old content after purging Cloudflare?

Because another cache is involved. Clear your browser cache (hard refresh), your WordPress caching plugin's cache, and any hosting-level cache in addition to Cloudflare. If it persists, verify you purged the correct URL and that Development Mode isn't masking the real cache state.

Should Cloudflare cache my WooCommerce checkout page?

No. Cart, checkout, my-account, and admin pages must never be cached, or customers will see broken sessions and failed orders. Create a Cache Rule to bypass cache on those URLs, and rely on Cloudflare's automatic bypass for logged-in and session cookies.

What does cf-cache-status DYNAMIC mean?

DYNAMIC means Cloudflare did not cache the response — the default behavior for HTML pages. It isn't an error. If you want those pages cached, create a "Cache Everything" rule for the appropriate URLs and set an Edge Cache TTL.

Final thoughts

Cloudflare cache problems feel mysterious but are almost always one of two things: an old copy that needs purging, or the wrong type of page being cached. Set up clean cache rules — cache static assets aggressively, bypass all dynamic pages, and purge automatically on updates — and most issues disappear for good. When something looks wrong, check cf-cache-status, clear all three cache layers, and you'll almost always find the culprit in minutes.

blog_posts.title: How to Fix a Slow WordPress Admin Dashboard
blog_posts.excerpt: Is your WordPress admin dashboard slow? Here is how to fix a sluggish wp-admin — trimming heavy plugins, taming the Heartbeat API, cleaning a bloated database, adding object caching, and upgrading PHP for a fast backend.
blog_posts.content:
A slow WordPress admin dashboard doesn't just annoy you — it quietly drains hours from every editing session. Pages take seconds to load, saving a post lags, and the whole backend feels sluggish. The frustrating part is that your public site can be fast while wp-admin crawls, because the admin area runs different, heavier processes. This guide shows you how to fix a slow WordPress admin dashboard, from the quick wins to the deeper causes.

Key takeaways

  • Admin-heavy plugins and the Heartbeat API are the two most common causes of a slow dashboard.
  • Object caching (Redis) and enough PHP memory dramatically speed up the backend.
  • A bloated database — especially autoloaded options — slows every admin page.
  • Query Monitor shows you exactly what's slowing things down.
  • The admin can be slow even when the front end is fast — it needs its own optimization.

First: diagnose with Query Monitor

Don't guess — measure. Install the free Query Monitor plugin, then open a slow admin page. It shows the slowest database queries, which plugins are consuming the most time, and any slow HTTP requests. In minutes it tells you exactly where the bottleneck is, so you fix the real cause instead of everything at once.

1. Deactivate or replace heavy admin plugins

Many plugins do most of their work in the dashboard — security scanners, analytics, backup tools, and page builders. One poorly optimized plugin can slow every admin page. Use Query Monitor to find the worst offenders, deactivate anything non-essential, and replace bloated plugins with lighter alternatives. This is usually the single biggest fix.

2. Control the WordPress Heartbeat API

The Heartbeat API powers autosave and real-time features by sending frequent requests to admin-ajax.php. On a busy dashboard, this constant polling can overload the server and slow everything down. Use the Heartbeat Control plugin to reduce its frequency or disable it where it isn't needed — a common and effective admin speed fix.

3. Increase your PHP memory limit

The admin area needs more memory than the front end. If yours is low, the dashboard lags. Raise it in wp-config.php — WordPress lets you set a separate, higher limit for the admin:

define( 'WP_MEMORY_LIMIT', '256M' );
define( 'WP_MAX_MEMORY_LIMIT', '512M' );

The WP_MAX_MEMORY_LIMIT value applies specifically to admin and background tasks, which are the most memory-hungry.

4. Clean up a bloated database

Over time your database fills with post revisions, expired transients, and orphaned data that slow every admin query. Clean it up with WP-Optimize and limit stored revisions by adding this to wp-config.php:

define( 'WP_POST_REVISIONS', 5 );

Fewer revisions and less junk data means faster post lists, edits, and dashboard loads.

5. Reduce autoloaded options

The wp_options table holds settings that WordPress loads on every page — including the admin. Plugins that leave behind large "autoloaded" options can silently bloat this and slow the whole site. Use Query Monitor or a database tool to find oversized autoloaded options and remove the ones left by old, deleted plugins.

6. Add object caching (Redis)

Object caching stores the results of repeated database queries in memory, which massively speeds up the admin area where the same queries run constantly. If your host supports Redis (many quality hosts do), enable it with a plugin like Redis Object Cache. It's one of the most effective backend speed upgrades available.

7. Trim dashboard widgets

The main dashboard screen loads widgets — some of which (like news feeds or plugin widgets) make external requests every time you log in, adding seconds to the load. Click Screen Options at the top of the dashboard and uncheck the widgets you don't use to lighten the page.

8. Update PHP and upgrade your hosting

An outdated PHP version makes everything slower. Make sure you're on PHP 8.0 or newer, which runs WordPress admin significantly faster. And remember: if you're on cheap, overcrowded shared hosting, no amount of tweaking fully fixes a slow backend. Quality hosting with proper resources is the foundation of a fast dashboard.

Frequently asked questions

Why is my WordPress admin dashboard so slow?

The most common causes are heavy admin plugins, the Heartbeat API polling too often, low PHP memory, and a bloated database with excessive autoloaded options. The admin area runs heavier processes than your public site, so it can be slow even when the front end is fast. Query Monitor helps pinpoint the exact cause.

What is the WordPress Heartbeat API and does it slow the admin?

The Heartbeat API sends regular requests to admin-ajax.php to power autosave and real-time features. When it fires too frequently, it can overload the server and slow the dashboard. Reducing its frequency with the Heartbeat Control plugin is a common way to speed up the admin.

Does object caching speed up the WordPress admin?

Yes, significantly. Object caching (such as Redis) stores repeated database query results in memory, which is especially helpful in the admin where the same queries run constantly. On a supported host, enabling Redis is one of the most effective backend speed improvements.

Can too many plugins slow down wp-admin?

Absolutely. Plugins that do heavy work in the dashboard — security scanners, analytics, backups, and page builders — add load to every admin page. It's less about the number of plugins than their weight; use Query Monitor to find and replace the slowest ones.

Final thoughts

A slow WordPress admin dashboard is almost always fixable once you know where to look. Start by diagnosing with Query Monitor, then tackle the usual causes: heavy plugins, the Heartbeat API, low memory, and a bloated database. Add object caching and keep PHP current, and your backend will feel fast again — turning every editing session from a waiting game into smooth, productive work.

blog_posts.title: WooCommerce Cart Not Updating
blog_posts.excerpt: WooCommerce cart not updating? It is almost always caching or a JavaScript/AJAX conflict. Here is how to fix it — exclude cart pages from caching, clear caches, and find the script conflict.
blog_posts.content:
A customer clicks "Add to Cart," and… nothing. The cart count stays at zero, the quantity won't change, or the totals don't refresh until they reload the page. A WooCommerce cart that won't update quietly kills sales — shoppers assume the store is broken and leave. The cause is almost always caching or a JavaScript conflict, and it's very fixable. Here's how I diagnose and solve it.

Quick answer: A WooCommerce cart that won't update is nearly always caused by caching the cart page or an AJAX/JavaScript conflict. Fix it by excluding the Cart, Checkout, and My Account pages from all caching, clearing every cache (plugin, server, CDN), and disabling the plugin or theme script that's breaking WooCommerce's AJAX. Enabling "AJAX add to cart" correctly usually seals it.

Why won't the WooCommerce cart update?

WooCommerce updates the cart dynamically with AJAX — it changes quantities and totals without a full page reload. Anything that interferes with that live request breaks the cart. The usual causes are:

  • Caching the cart — a cached (frozen) copy of the cart/checkout page is being served.
  • A JavaScript conflict — another plugin or the theme throws a JS error that stops WooCommerce's AJAX.
  • Aggressive optimization — minifying, combining, or deferring the wrong scripts breaks cart functionality.
  • A theme that isn't WooCommerce-compatible or is missing cart fragment support.
  • CDN or server caching ignoring WooCommerce's "don't cache" cookies.

Step 1: Exclude cart pages from caching (the #1 fix)

This solves the majority of cases. WooCommerce's dynamic pages must never be cached. In your caching plugin (WP Rocket, LiteSpeed, W3 Total Cache, etc.), add these to the "never cache" / cache exclusion list:

  • Cart
  • Checkout
  • My Account

Most caching plugins with WooCommerce support do this automatically — but verify it, because a misconfiguration here is the number one cause of a stuck cart. Also make sure caching respects the woocommerce_items_in_cart and woocommerce_cart_hash cookies.

Step 2: Clear every cache

After excluding the pages, purge all caches so the frozen version stops being served:

  • Your caching plugin's cache.
  • Your host/server cache (many managed WordPress hosts cache at server level).
  • Your CDN cache (Cloudflare, etc.) — and make sure Cloudflare isn't caching the cart via a page rule.
  • Your browser — hard refresh to be sure.

Test in an incognito window so you're seeing a fresh, uncached experience like a real customer.

Step 3: Check "AJAX add to cart" settings

Go to WooCommerce → Settings → Products and review the two AJAX options:

  • "Enable AJAX add to cart buttons on archives" — lets shoppers add from shop/category pages without a reload.
  • "Redirect to the cart page after successful addition" — if AJAX is misbehaving, temporarily enabling redirect confirms whether the item is actually being added (isolating an AJAX issue from a cart-storage issue).

If items add fine with redirect on but not with AJAX, you've confirmed the problem is a script conflict — move to Step 4.

Step 4: Find the plugin or script conflict

A JavaScript error from another plugin can stop WooCommerce's cart scripts cold. To find it:

  • Open your browser's developer console (F12) and watch for red JavaScript errors when you click "Add to Cart." The error usually names the guilty file/plugin.
  • Deactivate plugins one by one (especially other AJAX, cart, or optimization plugins), testing the cart after each.
  • In your optimization plugin, turn off minify/combine JavaScript and defer/delay JS for WooCommerce scripts — over-aggressive JS optimization is a classic cart-breaker.

Step 5: Test with a default theme

If plugins aren't the cause, your theme might be. Temporarily switch to a default theme like Storefront (WooCommerce's own theme) or Twenty Twenty-Four. If the cart works there, your theme is missing proper WooCommerce support or its cart fragments — contact the theme developer or add the missing AJAX fragment support.

Quick diagnosis table

Symptom Most likely cause & fix
Cart count doesn't change Cached cart page — exclude from caching & purge
Totals update only after reload AJAX/cart fragments blocked — check JS console
Quantity won't update JS conflict — deactivate plugins one by one
Broke after enabling optimization Disable JS minify/combine/defer for WooCommerce
Works on default theme only Theme missing WooCommerce cart fragments

Don't forget the basics

  • Update WooCommerce, your theme, and plugins — the bug may already be patched.
  • Confirm your theme declares WooCommerce support and includes cart fragments.
  • Check your SSL/site URL settings — mixed content (http vs https) can block AJAX requests.
  • Regenerate WooCommerce pages if the Cart/Checkout pages were deleted or reassigned.

Frequently asked questions

Why is my WooCommerce cart not updating?

The most common reasons are caching the cart page and JavaScript conflicts that block WooCommerce's AJAX. A cached copy of the cart shows stale totals, while a JS error from another plugin or aggressive optimization stops the cart from refreshing live. Excluding cart pages from caching and finding the script conflict fixes almost all cases.

Why does my cart only update after refreshing the page?

If the cart updates only on reload, WooCommerce's AJAX "cart fragments" are being blocked — usually by a caching layer serving a static page, or by minified/deferred JavaScript breaking the fragment request. Exclude the cart from caching and stop optimizing WooCommerce's cart scripts.

Should I cache the WooCommerce cart page?

No. The Cart, Checkout, and My Account pages are dynamic and personal to each visitor, so they should never be cached. Add them to your caching plugin's exclusion list; most WooCommerce-aware caching plugins do this automatically, but you should always verify.

How do I fix an "add to cart" button that does nothing?

Open your browser console (F12) and click the button — a red JavaScript error will usually name the conflicting plugin or script. Deactivate that plugin, and turn off JS minify/combine/defer for WooCommerce scripts in your optimization plugin. Test with a default theme to rule out theme issues.

Final thought

A WooCommerce cart that won't update is frustrating but rarely serious. In almost every case it comes down to caching the cart or a JavaScript conflict. Exclude the Cart, Checkout, and My Account pages from caching, purge every cache, check the browser console for JS errors, and dial back aggressive script optimization. Do that and your "Add to Cart" button will spring back to life — and stop leaking sales.

Cart still stuck and costing you orders? Get in touch and I'll fix your WooCommerce cart fast so customers can actually check out.
